ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba or Taiwan mazesoba. FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for mazesoba or Taiwan-style dry ramen. A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. A more specific composite dish code does not exist in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for mazesoba or Taiwan mazesoba as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: char siew (Chinese BBQ roasted pork / chashu)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Taiwan Mazesoba with Char Siew weighs approximately 450 g, comprising cooked ramen noodles, char siew pork slices, tare sauce, and toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle dish, composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked ramen noodles (FDC ID 168936), braised/roasted pork (FDC ID 167903), and soy-based sauce components; adapted for Taiwan mazesoba dry-style preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Taiwan Mazesoba exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of: cooked ramen-style wheat noodles (~55% by weight, USDA FDC 168936), char siew roasted pork (~25% by weight, USDA FDC 167903), soy tare sauce and condiments (~10%), and mixed toppings including nori, green onion, egg (~10%). Values are per 100 g of the combined dish as served. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, boiled — GI value 52 (±5); wheat noodle composite GI reference; analogous estimate applied to Taiwan mazesoba wheat ramen noodle base
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwan Mazesoba. GI estimated at 55 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles / instant noodles (GI ~52–58, medium category). The dish is predominantly wheat-based ramen noodles (~55% by weight). The protein and fat content from char siew and sauce components moderately attenuate glycemic response. GI of 55 and medium category are defensible analogous estimates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 101.25) / 100 ≈ 55.7, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 22.5 × 4.5 = 101.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based tare sauce and seasoned broth components
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving of Taiwan Mazesoba with Char Siew provides approximately 2,790 mg sodium, which substantially exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium sauce, and avoid adding extra soy sauce or chilli oil.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index wheat noodle base with moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Taiwan Mazesoba has a medium GI of approximately 55 and delivers around 101 g of carbohydrate per standard 450 g serving, resulting in a high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced portion (200–250 g), pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from char siew pork and elevated sodium from soy tare sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The char siew component contributes saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g) and the soy-based tare sauce contributes high sodium, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should limit serving frequency, choose leaner protein alternatives where available, and request low-sodium sauce options.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwan Mazesoba with Char Siew is a Japanese brothless dry ramen dish inspired by Taiwanese mixed noodles (ban-mian style). Unlike conventional ramen, mazesoba is served without soup; instead, thick wheat ramen noodles are tossed in a rich, umami-forward tare sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and chilli oil. The dish is topped with slices of char siew (Chinese-style BBQ roasted pork, known in Japanese as chashu), minced seasoned pork, a raw or soft-cooked egg yolk, nori (dried seaweed), green onions, and sometimes dried fish flakes (katsuobushi) or menma (bamboo shoots). The noodles are mixed vigorously at the table before eating, coating every strand in the concentrated sauce. The flavour profile is deeply savoury, slightly spicy, and rich, with a chewy noodle texture contrasting with tender pork. Nutritionally, the dish is calorie-dense (~195 kcal per 100 g), moderate in protein (~10.5 g per 100 g from pork and egg), and high in sodium (~620 mg per 100 g) due to the soy-based sauce. The medium glycemic index (~55) reflects the wheat noodle base, though the high carbohydrate load per full serving is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets due to pork content. In Singapore, Taiwan Mazesoba is served at specialty Japanese ramen restaurants such as Ramen Matsuri and is popular as a hearty lunch or dinner option.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as ramen?
What is the difference between mazesoba and ramen?
How many calories in Taiwan Mazesoba?
What is char siew in mazesoba?
What toppings come with Taiwan Mazesoba?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Taiwan Mazesoba with Char Siew exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) or in USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of approved USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked ramen noodles, roasted pork, and soy-based sauce components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled wheat noodles as the reference food. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source entry for Taiwan Mazesoba exists. 2. Sodium estimate (~620 mg per 100 g) may vary significantly depending on the restaurant's tare sauce formulation; actual sodium could be higher at some establishments. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite estimate. 5. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported across all component entries used for this composite estimate. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Taiwan Mazesoba has been published. 7. The input record note states 'Soup comes with all Mazesoba' — this is interpreted as an optional side soup (a common practice at mazesoba restaurants where a small soup is provided to add to the remaining sauce at the end); the dish itself is classified as brothless/dry-style. 8. The dish contains pork and is not halal-certified. 9. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual weight may vary by restaurant.
